title: Detecting non-trivial elements in the spaces of Legendrian knots via Algebraic K-theory
speaker: Yakov Eliashberg
affiliation: Stanford University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: This talk is based on a joint work with Thomas Kragh. Using the generating function theory we split inject homotopy groups of pseudo-isotopy and/or h-cobordism spaces into various spaces of Legendrian manifolds, e.g. the space of Legendrian unknots in ℝ2n+1 for a sufficiently large n. For instance, there is a non-trivial element in π2 of the space of Legendrian unknots in ℝ2n+1 for n≥12.

proof_stages.label: Pseudo-isotopy and h-cobordism input
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the goal of importing algebraic K-theory information into Legendrian topology, then reviews pseudo-isotopy spaces, their stabilization by adding quadratic variables, h-cobordism spaces, the equivalence between pseudo-isotopy and the loop space of h-cobordism, and the relevant connectivity theorem for inclusions.

proof_stages.label: Generating-function persistence framework
proof_stages.summary: Generating functions for Legendrians are reviewed, with the fibration-at-infinity and quadratic-stabilization conditions that make the theory usable, culminating in the persistence theorem that stabilized generating functions form a Serre fibration over the relevant Legendrian component.

proof_stages.label: Relative Legendrian detection of stable pseudo-isotopies
proof_stages.summary: A pseudo-isotopy function on a product such as M x R generates a Legendrian avoiding the zero wall, and the persistence theorem implies that a Legendrian null-homotopy would give a stabilized pseudo-isotopy null-homotopy, producing stable pseudo-isotopy classes inside homotopy groups of a relative Legendrian space.

proof_stages.label: Two unknottings and the loop construction
proof_stages.summary: The attempted insertion of a pseudo-isotopy into a zero section fails as a nearby-Lagrangian counterexample because it can be slid away, but comparing that unnotting with a Legendrian linear-homotopy unnotting produces loops of Legendrians and a map from pseudo-isotopy space to a loop space of Legendrians whose detected image is stabilization.

proof_stages.label: Delooping by a local h-cobordism action
proof_stages.summary: Using the identification of pseudo-isotopy with loops on h-cobordism, the loop construction is delooped by a local cusp/fold modification: the standard generating function z^3 - tz is replaced by z^3 - phi(t,x)z, where the zero set of phi is an h-cobordism, so the new Legendrian has that h-cobordism as its fold locus.

proof_stages.label: Detection map via the difference generating function
proof_stages.summary: For a Legendrian equipped with a generating function G, the off-diagonal difference function G plus (-G) has small regular level sets forming a stable h-cobordism, and local analysis shows that composing this detection map with the h-cobordism action gives the stabilization map.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and scope of the method
proof_stages.summary: Since stabilization is an isomorphism or split injection in the relevant stable range, algebraic K-theory and h-cobordism classes give nontrivial homotopy classes in spaces of Legendrian knots, with Whitehead-torsion classes affecting pi_0, while the present nontriviality proof relies on generating functions and does not yet extend to arbitrary contact or Lagrangian settings.

examples.name: Homogeneous polynomial with an isolated critical point
examples.note: The speaker mentions such polynomials as a nonessential way to visualize the fibration-at-infinity condition, after the quadratic-at-infinity case needed for stabilization has already been described.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites homogeneous polynomials with an isolated critical point as examples of functions that are fibrations at infinity, without analyzing one.

passes.reason: A homogeneous polynomial with an isolated critical point is used as a standard source of fibration-at-infinity generating functions, with no sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximal-symmetry feature invoked.
It is used as a standard family of functions with fibration at infinity, not to refute a claim.
Used only as a standard systematic class of functions giving fibrations at infinity, not as an adversarial example.

examples.name: Whitehead-torsion double of an h-cobordism
examples.note: A nontrivial h-cobordism whose double can be topologically trivial in even dimensions illustrates how Whitehead-group data can still change the Legendrian isotopy class produced by the construction.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through doubling a nontrivial h-cobordism, tracks the Whitehead torsion sign/dimension effect, and applies it to the Legendrian construction.

passes.reason: The even-dimensional double has cancelling Whitehead torsion and becomes a trivial h-cobordism, used constructively to keep topology unchanged while changing Legendrian isotopy class.
The even-dimensional double cancels Whitehead torsion to a trivial/product cobordism while being used to show unchanged topology can still give a different Legendrian isotopy class.
The mirror double has cancelling Whitehead torsion in the discussed case, giving a topologically trivial/product cobordism used to exhibit same topology but different Legendrian isotopy class.
In the even-dimensional use the doubled Whitehead torsion cancels to a trivial/product h-cobordism, showing topology can stay fixed while the Legendrian isotopy class changes.
the torsion-cancelling double is topologically a trivial/product h-cobordism, used to show unchanged topology can still yield a different Legendrian isotopy class.
The even-dimensional double cancels Whitehead torsion to give a topologically trivial h-cobordism while producing a nontrivial Legendrian class.
It is used constructively to produce Legendrian classes, and in the even-dimensional case discussed the double has canceled Whitehead torsion and is topologically the trivial product cobordism.
In the even-dimensional case used, the double cancels Whitehead torsion to a topologically trivial h-cobordism, serving to show topology can stay unchanged while the Legendrian isotopy class changes.
the even-dimensional double has its Whitehead torsion cancel, making the h-cobordism topologically trivial
The even-dimensional double cancels the Whitehead torsion to give a topologically trivial h-cobordism used constructively in the Legendrian construction.
the mirror double cancels Whitehead torsion/topology while producing a distinct Legendrian isotopy class
the even-dimensional double has cancelling Whitehead torsion/product topology and is used to show unchanged topology can still give a different Legendrian isotopy class

examples.name: Homeomorphic but nondiffeomorphic four-manifolds and cotangent bundles
examples.note: In response to a question about the nearby Lagrangian conjecture, the speaker uses this case to illustrate why existing gauge-theoretic or holomorphic-curve tools do not obviously settle such symplectic questions.
examples.engagement_reason: Homeomorphic but nondiffeomorphic four-manifolds and their cotangent bundles are raised only as a speculative nearby-Lagrangian-type problem, with no concrete work done on an instance.

passes.reason: Homeomorphic but nondiffeomorphic four-manifolds are exotic smooth-structure examples, raised as a possible hard case for cotangent-bundle symplectomorphism rather than a proven counterexample.
Homeomorphic but nondiffeomorphic four-manifolds are exotic smooth-structure examples raised as adversarial test cases for cotangent-bundle symplectic classification.
Exotic smooth four-manifold pairs are raised as adversarial potential cases for cotangent-bundle symplectomorphism/nearby-Lagrangian questions.
A homeomorphic but nondiffeomorphic 4-manifold is an exotic smooth-structure phenomenon, but it is raised as an open cotangent-bundle test case rather than an established disproof.
